perturbation.original_text: (d) As used in this Agreement, the term "Post-Term Period" shall mean a continuous uninterrupted period of two (2) years from the date of: (a) a transfer permitted under Section 14 of the Franchise Agreement; (b) expiration or termination of the Franchise Agreement (regardless of the cause for termination); (c) termination of Member's employment with Franchisee; and/or (d) a final order of a duly authorized arbitrator, panel of arbitrators, or a court of competent jurisdiction (after all appeals have been taken) with respect to any of the foregoing or with respect to the enforcement of this Agreement; either directly or indirectly (through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any persons, partnership, corporation or entity).
perturbation.changed_text: (d) As used in this Agreement, the term "Post-Term Period" shall mean a continuous uninterrupted period of five (5) years from the date of: (a) a transfer permitted under Section 14 of the Franchise Agreement; (b) expiration or termination of the Franchise Agreement (regardless of the cause for termination); (c) termination of Member's employment with Franchisee; and/or (d) a final order of a duly authorized arbitrator, panel of arbitrators, or a court of competent jurisdiction (after all appeals have been taken) with respect to any of the foregoing or with respect to the enforcement of this Agreement; either directly or indirectly (through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any persons, partnership, corporation or entity).
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-compete period to five years may be considered unreasonable in some jurisdictions, making it potentially unenforceable. Many states frown upon overly broad non-compete agreements, and a five-year period could be seen as excessive, particularly for lower-level employees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While there are some narrow exceptions, a broad five-year non-compete, especially for a lower-level employee, would likely be deemed invalid under California law as an unlawful restraint of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the non-compete period from two to five years, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly voids any contract restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business, stating that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While the law allows for exceptions, a broad five-year non-compete is likely unenforceable under California law as it constitutes an unlawful restraint of trade, reinforcing the law explanation. The change creates a direct conflict with the statute prohibiting restraints on trade.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Injunctive Relief. Member acknowledges that any failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement will cause Franchisor irreparable injury, and Member agrees to pay all court costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred by Franchisor in obtaining specific performance of, or an injunction against violation of, the requirements of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Injunctive Relief. Member acknowledges that any failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement will cause Franchisor irreparable injury, and Member agrees to pay all court costs and attorney's fees, regardless of reasonableness, incurred by Franchisor in obtaining specific performance of, or an injunction against violation of, the requirements of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the term "reasonable" from attorney's fees could lead to the contract requiring the member to pay excessive and unreasonable fees, which could be against public policy and disallowed by the courts. In many jurisdictions, attorney's fees must be reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1927 addresses sanctions for vexatious litigation and allows courts to impose costs, expenses, and attorney's fees on attorneys who unreasonably and vexatiously multiply proceedings. While this applies specifically to attorney conduct, removing the 'reasonableness' standard from the contract implies that any level of attorney's fees, no matter how excessive, would be recoverable. This can be viewed as against public policy which expects a degree of reasonableness in legal proceedings.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 28 / PART V / CHAPTER 123 / § 1927 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 28 USC 1927 : Counsel's liability for excessive costs Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 28-JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE PART V-PROCEDURE CHAPTER 123-FEES AND COSTS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments §1927. Counsel's liability for excessive costs Any attorney or other person admitted to conduct cases in any court of the United States or any Territory thereof who so multiplies the proceedings in any case unreasonably and vexatiously may be required by the court to satisfy personally the excess costs, expenses, and attorneys' fees reasonably incurred because of such conduct. ( June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 957 ; Pub. L. 96–34
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1927 - Counsel’s liability for excessive costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1927 - Counsel’s liability for excessive costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next Any attorney or other person admitted to conduct cases in any court of the United States or any Territory thereof who so multiplies the proceedings in any case unreasonably and vexatiously may be required by the court to satisfy personally the excess costs, expenses, and attorneys’ fees reasonably incurred because of such conduct. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 957 ; Pub. L. 96–349, § 3 , Sept. 12, 1980 , 94 Stat. 1156 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., § 829 (R.S. § 982). Word “personally” was inserted upon authority of Motion Picture Patents Co. v. Steiner et al., 1912, 201 F. 63, 119 C.C.A. 401. Reference to “proctor” was omitted as covered by the revised section. See definition of “court of the United States” in section 451 of this title . Changes were made in phraseology. Editorial Notes Amendments 1980— Pub. L. 96–349 substituted judicial authorization to require attorneys to satisfy excess costs, expenses, and attorneys’ fees reasonably incurred because of multiplication of proceedings for such prior authority to impose liability for increased costs based on multiplication of proceedings. perturbation.justification: The modification removes the term "reasonable" from "attorney's fees," potentially obligating the member to pay excessive fees. 28 U.S.C. § 1927, as shown in both snippets, addresses "Counsel's liability for excessive costs" and requires attorneys to satisfy "the excess costs, expenses, and attorneys' fees reasonably incurred." By removing 'reasonableness,' the changed text implies no limit on attorney's fees, contradicting the principle of reasonableness implied by the law snippets and potentially violating public policy.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Delay. No delay or failure by the Franchisor or Franchisee to exercise any right under this Agreement, and no partial or single exercise of that right, shall constitute a waiver of that or any other right provided herein, and no waiver of any violation of any terms and provisions of this Agreement shall be construed as a waiver of any succeeding violation of the same or any other provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Delay. No delay or failure by the Franchisor or Franchisee to exercise any right under this Agreement, and no partial or single exercise of that right, shall constitute a waiver of that or any other right provided herein, and no waiver of any violation of any terms and provisions of this Agreement shall be construed as a waiver of any succeeding violation of the same or any other provision of this Agreement. This clause applies regardless of statutory limitations periods.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "This clause applies regardless of statutory limitations periods" attempts to waive statute of limitations, which is often against public policy and not enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 360.5
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 360.5 specifies requirements for waiving the statute of limitations. Generally, a waiver must be in writing and signed. The changed text attempts to circumvent this requirement by stating the no-waiver clause applies regardless of statutory limitations, which is likely unenforceable and contradicts the statute.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 4. General Provisions as to the Time of Commencing Actions [350 - 363] ( Chapter 4 enacted 1872. ) 360.5. No waiver shall bar a defense to any action that the action was not commenced within the time limited by this title unless the waiver is in writing and signed by the person obligated. No waiver executed prior to the expiration of the time limited for the commencement of the action by this title shall be effective for a period exceeding four years from the date of expiration of the time limited for commencement of the action by this title and no waiver executed after the expiration of such time shall be effective for a period exceeding four years from the date thereof, but any such waiver may be renewed for a further period of not exceeding four years from the expiration of the immediately preceding waiver. Such waivers may be made successively. The provisions of this section shall not be applicable to any acknowledgment, promise or any form of waiver which is in writing and signed by the person obligated and given to any county to secure repayment of indigent aid or the re
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to bypass the statute of limitations by adding a clause that the 'no-waiver' provision applies regardless of statutory limitations periods. This directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure Section 360.5, which explicitly states that any waiver of the statute of limitations must be in writing and signed by the person obligated. The added clause aims to enforce a waiver without adhering to this mandatory requirement, thus creating a legal contradiction. The law snippet specifies, 'No waiver shall bar a defense to any action that the action was not commenced within the time limited by this title unless the waiver is in writing and signed by the person obligated,' which the added text directly undermines.
